Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty intense but also chaotic. I dedicated about 31 hours a week to studying, which sounds like a lot, but I had to spread it across five online courses. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time making notes, rewriting concepts, and creating summaries. I tried to block out specific times, but between my extracurriculars and the anxiety I deal with, things often shifted around. The mindfulness exercises I started doing helped me focus better when I actually sat down to work.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and how overwhelmed I was feeling. Some weeks I'd have a steady rhythm, studying in the mornings before things got hectic. But when deadlines piled up or my anxiety spiked, everything went out the window and I'd end up doing marathon sessions. I tried to keep some consistency, but life just doesn't always cooperate. The high stress made it hard to stick to any perfect schedule.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I remembered there was something due or when I got a notification about new content. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important because my attendance wasn't great and I worried about falling behind. The anxiety actually pushed me to check the LMS more frequently than I might have otherwise. I also logged in when I needed to watch lecture videos I'd missed from not attending in person.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks, checking the LMS became almost automatic, especially in the evenings. I'd finish dinner, do some mindfulness breathing, and then open up the platform to see what needed attention. It became part of my wind-down routine weirdly enough. Having five courses meant there was always something happening on there, so it just made sense to check regularly. The high engagement kind of happened naturally because I couldn't afford to miss things.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful for me since I missed a lot of classes in person. I watched almost 200 minutes of video content, which really helped fill in the gaps. The reading materials were also essential because of my learning style—I could download them, annotate them, and really process the information. Discussion posts were helpful too, even though I only posted three times, because reading others' perspectives helped clarify concepts I was struggling with.
Q6: I'll be honest, I often delayed the peer interaction stuff beyond what was strictly necessary. I did engage with 16 peer interactions, but there were probably more opportunities I skipped. Sometimes the optional quizzes or practice exercises got pushed aside when I was stressed. I also ignored some of the supplementary videos that seemed repetitive. When you're managing high anxiety and five courses, you have to triage what's essential versus nice-to-have.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to assignments or exams. If I knew something would help me understand a concept I was struggling with, I was all in. The activities that let me read and write—like taking notes on lectures or working through written problems—felt most valuable to me. I also appreciated anything that helped reduce my anxiety about upcoming assessments. If it felt like busywork with no clear purpose, I struggled to justify the time.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. Required ones had this weight to them—I knew they'd impact my grade, so even when my anxiety was through the roof, I pushed through. Optional stuff I'd only do if I genuinely felt lost on a topic or if I had a rare moment of calm. There was also less guilt about skipping optional things, which honestly was a relief. Sometimes I'd save optional activities for when I needed a break from the pressure of graded work.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and readings, then work through them with a notebook beside me, writing and rewriting key points. I also used external YouTube videos to supplement concepts that weren't clicking. My textbooks got a lot of use too, especially when the online materials felt insufficient. The LMS was really just the starting point—the real learning happened with my notes spread across my desk.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or working through practice problems from the textbook, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also have study sessions with a couple of classmates where we'd quiz each other verbally. Sometimes I'd re-read downloaded materials multiple times without ever logging back in. My best thinking often happened while walking and mentally reviewing concepts, which obviously leaves no digital trace.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF or watch a video once, then work with that material for days without touching the LMS again. My read-write style means I need to process things slowly, annotate them, and return to them multiple times. It was more efficient to have everything saved locally than to keep logging in. Plus, when my internet was being unreliable or I was studying somewhere without good connection, having offline materials was essential.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything seemed to pile up at once. My anxiety would spike, and even though I have pretty high resilience, sometimes it felt like too much. When I missed too many classes in a row, catching up felt overwhelming and I'd freeze a bit. Personal stuff and extracurricular commitments also competed for my energy. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me push through those harder moments.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or had competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars, my LMS usage became very targeted—just checking what was absolutely necessary. Confusion about course material sometimes made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to face how behind I felt. Fatigue was a big one; after a long day, even opening the platform felt like a chore. I learned to work in shorter bursts when I was depleted rather than forcing long sessions.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd be scrolling through materials but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd watch a video and realize I hadn't absorbed anything because I was too anxious about other deadlines. There were times I'd have the LMS open while technically doing other things, so it looked like engagement but wasn't really. When stress was at its peak, I'd go through the motions without the material actually sinking in. Quality of engagement definitely varied.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, which is why my completion rate wasn't great even though my scores were decent when I did submit. Sometimes the anxiety made starting feel impossible, so I'd avoid it until the pressure became unbearable. Discussion posts were something I'd delay because they required a different kind of energy than just reading. I knew these things were important, but knowing and doing are different when you're struggling with stress.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary driver for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active—watching videos I'd put off, downloading readings, checking rubrics. Without deadlines, I probably would have engaged even less consistently. They created structure that my anxious brain actually needed, even though they also caused stress. My usage patterns probably looked very spiky, with bursts of activity right before due dates.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback on my assignment scores, it motivated me to engage more. Seeing that I scored well despite my struggles validated that my methods were working. Negative or constructive feedback made me revisit materials and try harder, though it also sometimes increased my anxiety. I wish there had been more frequent feedback because it helped me gauge whether I was on track. The gap between submitting and hearing back was always stressful.
Q18: The course structure had a big impact. When modules were clearly organized and I knew exactly what was expected, I engaged more consistently. Having five courses meant I relied heavily on clear structure to manage everything. Courses with predictable patterns—like weekly readings followed by discussions—were easier to fit into my routine. When structure was loose or unclear, my anxiety made me second-guess everything and I'd either over-engage or avoid it entirely.
Q19: Clear deadlines and well-organized modules made engagement much easier. The video content being available to watch anytime was crucial since my attendance was low. What made things harder was when instructions were vague or when there were too many small tasks that felt fragmented. Having resources downloadable was a huge help for my learning style. The discussion forums could have been better structured to encourage more meaningful interaction.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is searching for understanding but struggling to find it. They might watch videos multiple times, click through different resources, trying to grasp something that isn't clicking. It could also be anxiety-driven—checking and rechecking without actually completing work because starting feels too overwhelming. Or maybe they're gathering information but doing the actual synthesis and writing offline. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My attendance was really low and my task completion wasn't great, but I still passed and my assignment scores were solid. I did a lot of learning outside the LMS—reading textbooks, making notes, studying with peers. Some students are just efficient or have strong foundational knowledge that lets them perform well without constant platform engagement. The LMS is a tool, not the only path to learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend on pages. They're good at capturing frequency and timing of engagement. But they miss the quality of that engagement, whether you're actually learning or just going through motions. They can't see offline studying, mental processing, or the emotional state behind the clicks. They definitely can't capture growth in understanding or the effort it takes to push through anxiety to engage at all.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious about what I'm learning, when concepts start connecting in my mind. For me, it involves active processing—writing notes, asking questions, applying ideas. It's also about showing up mentally even when it's hard, which given my anxiety levels, sometimes feels like a victory in itself. Engagement is internal as much as it is about visible activity.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought through anxiety to complete things, or the mindfulness practices that made my studying possible. It wouldn't capture the hours of offline note-taking and textbook reading that were central to my learning. It can't see the peer conversations, the mental rehearsal, or the emotional labor of managing stress while trying to succeed. My resilience and the internal work I did to stay motivated despite everything—that's invisible in the data but essential to my story.
